segments.s451-g25: This phrase lists two sources introduced by the preposition ز (z, “from”), which precedes the noun it governs (صندوق). The second item is written وز but should be read as و + ز (‘and from’); و (and) links the two 'from' phrases. شیرخوار (suckling) qualifies کودک (infant), with the modifier following the head noun as is common in Persian.
segments.s451-g26: Parallel structure to the previous segment: ز (from) governs دینار (dinar). Again وز stands for و + ز (‘and from’). گوهر (gem) is modified by شاهوار (kingly/royal) that follows the noun in postnominal adjective position.
segments.s451-g27: بدو is the prepositional phrase meaning ‘to him’ (به او) placed before the verb; گفت here functions as the verb ‘said’ (classical past), followed by the speaker’s self-identification ما دستکاران (we, the workmen/artisans). بدیم in this context is an archaic or poetic form related to دادن (‘we give’) rather than the adjective بد; its literal sense is determined by the clause (but the listed senses did not include ‘give’).
segments.s451-g28: نه (not) negates the following prepositional phrase از تخمه کامکاران; از is the ordinary preposition ‘from/of’, تخمه here means ‘offspring/lineage’ and کامکاران ‘the prosperous/fortunate ones’. بدیم repeats the archaic verb-form ‘we give’ (negated by نه) — meaning ‘we do not give from the stock/lineage of the fortunate’. The clause denies noble/fortunate provenance.
segments.s451-g29: ازان (از آن) ‘from that / as for that’ introduces what follows; تو is the indirect object ‘for you’. داریم is present ‘we have’ and چیزی که هست is a relative construction ‘something that is (there/available)’. Word order is typical Persian: possessor/beneficiary (تو) follows the prepositional phrase and precedes the verb.
segments.s451-g30: ز introduces the thing being specified: پوشیدنی جامه is a noun phrase meaning ‘a garment for wearing’ (پوشیدنی modifying جامه). و connects the next verb; برنشست is a past verb ‘sat upon’ or idiomatically ‘put on (over one’s body)’ — here it follows and completes the action of giving by reporting that he put the garment on.
segments.s451-g31: پرستنده ماییم is a nominative assertion ‘we are (your) devotees/adorers’ with the copula fused into ماییم (we are). و links the next clause; فرمان تراست is a classical/poetic verbless clause meaning ‘the command/decision is yours’ (تراست = ‘it is yours’). The possessive sense of تراست assigns authority to the addressee.
segments.s451-g32: نگر is an imperative ‘consider/see’. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (نگر تا چه … = ‘see/consider what…’). چه is the interrogative/indefinite ‘what’, باید is the modal ‘must/ought’, and تن و جان are the coordinated nouns ‘body and soul/life’; تراست repeats تراست (‘it shall be yours’), so the clause asks the addressee to judge what should belong to him in body and life.
segments.s451-g1: This fronted phrase combines a dative prepositional phrase and a reporting verb plus temporal marker. به گازر = 'to the passer/passerby' (به + indirect object). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین is an adverbial 'thus' and گفت is the past tense reporting verb). روزی که = 'one day that/when' (روزی = temporal adverb, که introduces the reported subordinate clause), and من is the subject of that subordinate clause ('I'). Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + reported-verb + temporal phrase + complementizer + subject.
segments.s451-g2: This is the reported content clause. همی is a frequentative/continuous adverb (poetic) meaning 'always/still' and sits before the noun phrase. این نهان = 'this secret' (این = 'this', نهان = 'hidden/secret'), دارم = 'I hold/I keep' (present-tense possessive/active sense of داشتن). از انجمن = 'from the assembly' (از marks source). The verb دارم appears at the end, consistent with Persian clause-final verb placement in narrative.
segments.s451-g3: نجنبد is a jussive/optative form (present stem with ن- negation) meaning 'may it not move/waver' (classical poetic use). همی again adds continuous force 'always/ever'. بر تو = 'on/upon you' and بر مهر من = 'with respect to my love / upon my affection' (مهر = 'love/affection'). Together this yields the wish 'may my love for you never stir/let it never waver in you.'
segments.s451-g4: نماند here functions as an optative/present negated form 'may not cease / may remain' (in context read idiomatically as a wish about continuance). به چهر تو = 'on/in your face/appearance' (به marks location), هم = 'also / likewise' linking the two faces, چهر من = 'my face/appearance.' The phrase expresses reciprocal presence of faces/affection; verb-final ordering is preserved and هم links the items.
segments.s451-g5: شگفت آیدم is an impersonal/psychological construction: literally 'wonder comes to me' = 'I am astonished.' چون introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' or 'since.' پسر خوانیم = 'we call (him) a son' (خوانیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive/indicative 'we call/summon'; object pronoun is omitted but understood). The clause therefore means 'I am astonished when we call him a son.'
segments.s451-g6: به دکان = 'to/at the shop' (به marks goal/location). بر خویش ش (spelled here as بر خویش ش split across tokens) = 'on/at his own (place)' — خویش = 'one's own' (here expressed by خوی + enclitic ش = his/her). بنشانیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive/optative 'we seat/place' and appears sentence-finally: 'we seat him in his own shop.' Note the poetic split of خویش into خوی + ش.
segments.s451-g7: بدو is the dative/clitic-like form 'to him' (to/unto him). گفت گازر = 'the passer said' (word order places the verb before the noun). که introduces the reported content 'that,' and اینت (an archaic/dialectal demonstrative) + سخن = 'this speech/this talk.' The sequence reports what the passer told him.
segments.s451-g8: دریغ آن شده is a common classical formula meaning 'it is regrettable / it has come to regret that...' (دریغ = pity/regret; آن = 'that'; شده = 'has become' or 'has happened'). رنجهای کهن = 'the ancient/old sufferings/troubles' (رنجهای plural of رنج, کهن = old/ancient). The clause therefore says that, regrettably, the old sufferings have arisen/been brought back.
segments.s451-g81: 'نماید که' is an impersonal/predictive phrase meaning 'it appears that' (an apparent/experiential present used in narrative). 'این نامداری' is a nominal phrase 'this renown / this famed one' (این = demonstrative modifying نامداری). 'بود' is the past copula indicating state ('was'). Word order is typical Persian: verb final 'بود' follows the nominal predicate.
segments.s451-g82: A nominal predicate consisting of two adjectives linked to a noun: 'خردمند و جنگی' modify the head noun 'سواری' (horseman). 'و' simply coordinates the adjectives. 'بود' is the past copula ('was'), so the whole clause reads 'was a wise and warlike horseman.'
segments.s451-g83: A present-tense predicate describing qualities: the adjectives 'دلیر و سرافراز و کنداور' are coordinated and attribute to the (understood) subject 'he'. 'است' is the present copula 'is'. In classical style multiple adjectives precede the copula.
segments.s451-g84: 'ولیکن' is a conjunction 'but'. 'سلیحش' is the noun 'سلیح' (weapon/armament) with the enclitic possessive '-ش' = 'his'. 'نه اندرخور' is a negated predicate meaning 'not worthy/suitable', where 'اندرخور' is a predicate adjective and 'است' is the copula 'is'. Word order places the negator before the predicate.
segments.s451-g85: 'چو' here means 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). 'داراب را' marks Darab as the person involved (را topicalizes/marks the referent). 'فرمند آمدش' should be read as 'Farmand came to him' (poetic word order: subject/name appears before the verb 'آمدش' = 'came to him'). The whole gives 'When Farmand came to Darab.'
segments.s451-g86: 'سپه را' topicalizes 'the army'. 'سراسر پسند آمدش' is an idiom: 'پسند آمدش' = 'it came as pleasing to him' = 'he liked it', with 'سراسر' 'entirely/completely' modifying the liking. 'آمدش' again is 'came to him' functioning with 'پسند' to express 'it pleased him.'
segments.s451-g87: 'ز اختر' is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/by the stars' implying astrological fate. 'یکی روزگاری' is a temporal phrase 'on a certain day' ('یکی' = a certain, 'روزگاری' = time/day). 'گزید' is the past verb 'chose' (the subject is implicit: fate/fortune).
segments.s451-g88: 'ز بهر' marks purpose/benefit 'for' (here 'for the office of'). 'سپهبد' names the office/rank (spahbed). 'چنان چون سزید' is a fixed-like phrase meaning 'in such a way as was fitting / as he deserved' — 'چنان' and 'چون' reinforce similarity and 'سزید' is used in the sense 'to deserve / be fitting'.
segments.s451-g41: This is a compact past statement: بدو (‘to him’) is the indirect object, داد is the main past verb ‘gave’, دینار چندانک is an amount phrase ‘dinars (so many)’ and بود functions as a past auxiliary/emphatic copula ('had been/gave' or 'was so many'). Word order is poetic (indirect object precedes verb, auxiliary at the end).
segments.s451-g42: بماند is a past-form meaning ‘remained/was left’; آن گران گوهر is the noun phrase ‘that costly gem’ and نابسود is a predicative adjective ‘fruitless / of no avail’. The verb precedes the noun phrase (poetic order) and نابسود qualifies the state of the gem.
segments.s451-g43: به دینار is an instrumental/prepositional phrase ‘with/by dinars’, اسپی is the direct object ‘a steed’, خرید is the past verb ‘bought’, او is the pronoun ‘he’, and پسند here is postpositive/attributive meaning ‘pleasing to him / which he liked’. The clause literally reads ‘with dinars a steed bought he, pleasing to him.’
segments.s451-g44: This is a balanced coordinate phrase with an omitted copula: یکی … و دیگر … = ‘one … and the other …’. یکی کم‌بها زین = ‘one (was) a low‑valued / cheap saddle’; و دیگر کمند = ‘and the other (was) a halter/lasso’. The verb 'was' is understood and omitted in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s451-g45: یکی introduces one person; مرزبان بود is the nominal statement ‘was a marzban’. با introduces accompanying/possessed items 'with', so با سنگ و رای means ‘with gems and counsel’ (possession or qualities). Word order places the possessional phrase after the predicate, typical in poetic narrative.
segments.s451-g46: A string of adjectival/nominative attributes: بزرگ و پسندیده و رهنمای = ‘great and approved/esteemed and a guide’. Adjectives and the final noun (رهنمای) serve to qualify the marzban; conjunctions و merely link the qualities.
segments.s451-g47: خرامید is a past verb ‘strode/pranced proudly’; it appears before its subject داراب (poetic verb‑subject order). نزدیک اوی is a locative phrase ‘near him’, where اوی is the archaic/poetic third‑person pronoun meaning ‘him’.
segments.s451-g48: A cluster of predicates describing his state: پراندیشه ‘full of thought/worried’, بد جان ‘ill‑of‑heart’ (بد used as ‘ill/poorly’ with جان), تاریک اوی ‘dark/gloomy was he’ — اوی again is the archaic pronoun. Adjectives and nouns stack to give a compact psychological portrait.
segments.s451-g65: This is a temporal clause headed by "چون" (= “as/when”) showing cause/time: "سپه چون فراوان شد" = "when the host became numerous." Word order is Verb-final in the subordinate clause: subject سپه + subordinating چون + adjective فراوان + past verb شد. The prepositional phrase "از هر دری" (= “from every door/gate”) follows and specifies origin.
segments.s451-g66: "همی آمد" uses the archaic/poetic progressive particle همی + past آمد to mean an ongoing or repeated action “kept coming.” The phrase "از هر سوی لشکری" (= “troops from every direction”) uses از + هر سوی to indicate source/direction, with لشکری as a noun (troops) modified by the preceding locative.
segments.s451-g67: The verb بیامد (past "came") precedes its source phrase ز کاخ همایون ("from the auspicious/imperial palace"). The following noun همای is the subject that came; in classical Persian the verb-initial order is common and the nominal subject can follow the verb and its modifiers.
segments.s451-g68: This is a coordinated subject phrase: خود (reflexive/emphatic "himself") plus مرزبانان ("border-guards") modified by the adjective پاکیزه‌رای ("clean/neat of countenance"). The conjunction و links the two coordinated items; the whole phrase completes who/what "came" in the previous segment.
segments.s451-g69: بدان تا is a classical connective meaning roughly “so that” or “with the result that.” It introduces a purposive/result clause with the subjunctive بگذرند (they may pass). سپه is the subject, preceded by the temporal/purpose marker; پیش او ("before him/in his presence") is the locative phrase. The verb بگذرند is subjunctive (plural) marking potential or intended action.
segments.s451-g70: This is the object list of the verb بشمرند: a coordinated series of nouns "تن و نام و دیوانها" ("bodies and names and the registers/rolls") placed before the verb, as is common in classical Persian. بشمرند is subjunctive/plural here, matching the implied agents of counting.
segments.s451-g71: همی بود چندی places the progressive particle همی with the past verb بود to indicate a continued state (“remained/was for a while”). چندی (= “for a while”) gives duration. بران (archaic locative) + پهن دشت (= “on that broad plain”) specifies the location of that continued state.
segments.s451-g72: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction “when.” "لشکر فراوان" is the subject "the numerous host," and برو برگذشت is a compound verbal sequence meaning “went on/passed by” (برو = go, برگذشت = passed). The clause therefore reads “when the abundant army passed by.”
segments.s451-g57: یکی before a noun is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral 'one') and introduces an unspecified person. مرد is the noun 'man'; بد نام is a two‑word adjectival phrase meaning 'ill‑reputed' (بد modifies نام; literally 'bad name' = bad reputation). او is the third‑person pronoun referring back to that man, and the final token رشنواد is a proper name (postposed here, as is common in poetic Persian, effectively apposition: 'a man ... — Rashnavad').
segments.s451-g58: سپهبد is a title (spahbed, high military commander). بد again qualifies it as 'ill‑famed' or 'bad'; the pronoun او again picks out the same person. هم is the additive 'also/too' linking this clause to the previous, and سپهبدنژاد is a name/epithet (literally 'of spahbed‑lineage') used as his surname/epithet. Word order is appositive: title + epithet, then the explanatory name.
segments.s451-g59: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb used here to mean 'he ordered/commanded' (classical Persian uses such forms for royal speech). تا introduces a purpose or result clause ('so that / in order that'). برکشد is an older poetic form meaning 'to advance/march/lead (forth)'; here it appears in the subordinate (jussive/purpose) clause. سوی + روم is the prepositional phrase 'toward Rome' (روم = Byzantium/the Romans).
segments.s451-g60: به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword' (به functioning as the instrumental marker here). ویران کند is a verb phrase where ویران ('ruined/devastated') combines with the light verb کند ('to make/do') to mean 'to lay waste' or 'to make desolate'; in classical/poetic Persian such adjective+کند forms a transitive verbal sense. روی بوم is the object/place phrase 'the face/surface of the land' = 'the homeland/land itself' (روی = 'face/upon', بوم = 'homeland').
segments.s451-g61: سپه گرد کرد is a verbal construction meaning 'assembled/mustered an army' — سپه 'army' plus گرد (here verbal/collective sense 'to gather') plus the light verb کرد 'did/made'. آن زمان is the temporal adverbial 'at that time' (postposed here), and the final رشنواد is the subject's name placed after the clause for emphasis (typical of poetic word order: verb phrase followed by time and then the actor's name).
segments.s451-g62: عرض‌گاه بنهاد is 'he set up/established an audience‑place' (عرض‌گاه = place for display/holding court; بنهاد = he founded/established). و is the coordinating 'and'. روزی here is used nounally ('a day' or 'a time/occasion' — classical Persian often uses روزی in such senses), and بداد in older/political register can function as 'he gave/set' (though the lemma list shows a different meaning; in context it means 'appointed/set a day' or 'gave a provision/appointed an occasion'). So the clause means he established the place and appointed a day/occasion.
segments.s451-g63: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. داراب is the proper name 'Darab' as the subject of the main clause. شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became', and شادکام is the predicate adjective 'joyful/fortunate' (so literally 'when he heard, Darab became joyful').
segments.s451-g64: به نزدیک او رفت is 'he went near him' (به + نزدیک = toward/near; او = him; رفت = went). و coordinates the next action. بنوشت here is a past action 'he wrote/inscribed' (from نوشتن with a poetic prefix b-), and نام is the object 'name' (so 'he went up to him and wrote/inscribed the name'). Note: some lexical lists show بنوشت as a different lemma, but in this context it functions as the past of 'to write/inscribe'.
segments.s451-g49: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb (classical/poetic) placed before the verb to mark ongoing or repeated action. داشتش is داشت + enclitic object -ش (’him/it’) and here functions as a past/habitual verb meaning 'kept/was keeping (him)'; the subject مرزبان ارجمند ('the noble marzban') follows the verb, a normal option in Persian poetic word order, with ارجمند modifying مرزبان.
segments.s451-g50: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گیتی is its object 'the world/earth'. نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' used impersonally here (no harm came). بروبر is an adverb/idiom meaning 'directly/face‑to‑face' and modifies گزند, which is the noun 'harm' placed after the verb — a common verb‑first order in older/epic Persian: 'from the world did not come direct harm.'
segments.s451-g51: چنان بد is a degree phrase 'so/so bad'; که introduces a result clause ('that...'). آمد is the past 'came' and سپاهی ('an army/host') is the subject (here placed after or near the verb, as is common). ز روم 'from Rûm' specifies the origin of that army.
segments.s451-g52: به غارت is an adverbial phrase 'for/to plunder' (به + noun 'غارت'). بران is a deictic/adverbial 'on/thereupon/that' linking to the following noun phrase. مرز آباد بوم is a stacked noun phrase 'the prosperous/inhabited border land' (مرز 'border', آباد 'prosperous/inhabited' as adjective, بوم 'land/homeland').
segments.s451-g53: به رزم is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in battle'; اندرون 'within/inside' further localizes the action. مرزبان is the semantic subject and کشته شد is a passive verbal phrase made of the participle کشته ('killed') plus شد as auxiliary, yielding 'was killed'. The elements before the verb are fronted adverbials, then the affected agent (مرزبان) and the passive predicate.
segments.s451-g54: سر لشکرش is a genitival phrase 'the head/leader of his army' (سر 'head/chief' + لشکر with enclitic -ش 'his'). زان سخن is a circumstantial phrase 'from/at that word/news' (زان = 'from that'; سخن = 'word/news/report' here). گشته شد is a compound: گشته (perfect/participial form 'become/been turned') + شد (auxiliary 'was'), producing 'was changed/was struck/was affected' — in context a classical idiom meaning 'was overcome/dismayed by the news.'
segments.s451-g55: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی here means 'information/news' and آمد is the simple past 'came/reached'. به نزد is the classical prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of' and همای is the proper name 'Homay' (the queen) as the recipient of the news.
segments.s451-g56: که introduces the content clause ('that...'). رومی('s') refers to the Byzantines/Rûm. نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' and here combines with پای ('foot') in the idiom نهاد پای = 'set foot'. اندرین 'in this/among these' modifies مرز 'border', so the clause says 'that the Romans had set foot on this frontier.'
segments.s451-g33: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is a past (perfect) third-person singular 'he heard' and, in poetry, the subject داراب follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). خیره بماند is a compound predicate: خیره is a predicative adverb/adjective 'staring/astonished' and بماند (poetic form) means 'remained/stood' (third-person), so together 'stood staring/was left astonished.'
segments.s451-g34: روان را is the direct object ('his soul / his mind') marked by the object marker را. به اندیشه اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into/unto thought' (به + noun + archaic preposition اندر 'in/into'), and نشاند is an archaic transitive verb meaning 'caused to sit / placed' (i.e. 'set/put'); together 'he set his soul into thought' or 'he fell into thought.'
segments.s451-g35: بدو گفت is a light ditransitive pattern 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him' before the verb). زین خواسته = 'from this request' (زین is an archaic form meaning 'from this' / 'از این'), هیچ ماند = 'nothing remained' (هیچ = 'nothing/no', ماند = 'remained' or 'is left', here negating the remainder).
segments.s451-g36: وگر is a conditional 'but if' / 'and if'. گازر here functions as 'report' / 'what is told' (an agent of spreading news), آن را is the object 'that' + object marker, همه 'entirely/all', and برفشاند is a transitive verb 'to scatter/throw upon' (here meaning 'it will all be scattered' or 'it will all be dispersed' if the matter is reported). Word order places the object before the verb, as normal in Persian.
segments.s451-g37: که is a complementizer 'that'. باشد is the subjunctive/present-form 'be' (here stating a hypothetical or evaluative clause). بهای 'price/value' governed by the following noun, and یکی بارگی is an idāfa-like grouping 'of one leaf' (یکی = 'one', بارگی = 'leaf/foliage'), so بهای یکی بارگی = 'the value/price of a single leaf.'
segments.s451-g38: بدین روز is an adverbial phrase 'on this day' formed from a prefixed ب (be- / 'on/to') + an archaic/variant token for 'this' + روز 'day' (classical word order). کندی و بیچارگی are two coordinated nouns 'slowness (or delay) and misery/helplessness' that serve as the predicate/complement of باشد earlier ('should be slowness and misery').
segments.s451-g39: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave' used with پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the reported content 'that'. بیش است ازین is 'is more than this' (بیش = 'more', است = 'is', از = 'than/from', ین = an orthographic/poetic form of 'این' = 'this').
segments.s451-g40: درخت برومند و باغ و زمین is a coordinated noun phrase listing items whose value is being compared: درخت 'tree', برومند is a postpositive adjective 'prosperous/ flourishing' modifying درخت, then و باغ و زمین 'and garden and land'. The adjective after the noun is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s451-g73: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause (چو + past verb = 'when he saw'). داراب را is the direct object with the postposition را marking definiteness/emphasis; the verb دید is past tense 'saw'. با + two nouns (فر و برز) is a prepositional phrase 'with X and Y' listing qualities that accompany Darab (فر = divine/royal glory, برز = exaltedness).
segments.s451-g74: به گردن برآورده is a resultative/locative phrase meaning 'having placed/put around the neck' (به + noun phrase + participial/past form). پولاد گرز is a noun phrase 'steel mace' where پولاد modifies گرز. The past participial/preterite form برآورده functions here to show the action/result (he had it placed/hung).
segments.s451-g75: تو گفتی is the simple past 'you said' (subject pronoun + past verb). The reported clause follows: همه دشت پهنای اوست is a verbless/equational clause where پهنای او ('his breadth') is predicated as 'همه دشت' ('the whole plain'); ست is the copula 'is' placed after the predicate in classical style.
segments.s451-g76: This is a parallel equational statement: زمین زیر پوینده بالای اوست. زمین ('the ground/earth') + زیر ('under/beneath') + پوینده (present participle 'the one who moves/ the marcher') form 'the earth is beneath the marcher'. بالای اوست ('his height/above is') uses بالای + pronoun + copula to assert his loftiness. Word order places the locative phrase (زیر پوینده) after the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s451-g77: چو + دید again makes a temporal clause 'when he saw'. آن modifies the coordinated nouns بر و چهره (i.e. 'that ... and face'), so 'that glory and (that) pleasant face'; note that determiners like آن can scope over a coordinated pair. چهره دلپذیر is a noun + adjective phrase 'pleasant/agreeable countenance.'
segments.s451-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/at' introducing the source (ز پستان مادر = 'at/from the mother's breast'). The verb بپالود is a past-form verb here taking شیر ('lion') as its subject; syntactically the noun شیر follows the verb (common in poetry) so the line reads 'the lion was suckled/nursed at its mother's breast.'
segments.s451-g79: بپرسید is a past-tense third-person verb 'he asked' (or 'they asked'), followed by و گفت 'and said', which introduces the reported question or speech. این سوار از کجاست is an embedded interrogative meaning 'where is this rider from?' — note the copula ست appears within the question word cluster (کجاست) as in colloquial/classical Persian.
segments.s451-g80: بدین شاخ و این برز و بالای راست is a string of noun phrases joined by و describing distinguishing attributes. بدین here functions as 'with this' (ب + demonstrative sense), modifying شاخ 'horn'; the sequence names features ('this horn and this exaltation') and ends with بالای راست ('the high/right place' or 'right-hand superiority'). The verbless fragment completes the reported description started by گفت.
segments.s451-g9: تراگر is a fused, archaic conditional address meaning “if (for) you”; the clause that follows states a comparison. منش here means “origin/lineage” and zان (زان) = “from that.” Word order is topical/poetic: the contrasted topic تراگر منش زان (if your origin is from that) precedes the main predication من برتر است (“mine is superior”), where من is the subject and برتر است is a simple present/past-tense (=classic narrative) predicate “is superior.”
segments.s451-g10: پدرجوی را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker (“as for the one who seeks his father”); راز is the nominal predicate “secret.” با + مادر is a prepositional phrase “with the mother,” and است is the copula. The whole clause means “As for the paternity‑seeker, the secret is with the mother.”
segments.s451-g11: چنان بد که … is a result/extent construction “so/that …”: چنان (so) + بد (bad/ill), with که introducing the clause of result. یک روز گازر is the temporal phrase + subject (“one day the passer”), and برفت is the past verb “he went/left” (classical past form). The subject precedes the verb in normal Classical Persian order.
segments.s451-g12: ز خانه سوی رود is a directional prepositional chain “from the house toward the river.” یازید is a past verb meaning to burst/open forth in this poetic context and تفت qualifies the manner (a poetic adjective/adverb; here conveying fierce/fiery action). The verb follows its adverbial phrase, yielding “he burst forth from the house toward the river.”
segments.s451-g13: در here is the noun “door/gate” (not the preposition), followed by خانه (house) and the object marker را marking “the door (of the house)” as definite. تنگ modifies the closure (“tightly/closely”). داراب (proper name) is the subject placed before the verb, and بست is the past transitive verb “closed.” The line therefore reads in natural order as “Darab closed the house‑door tightly.”
segments.s451-g14: بیامد is the past tense “he came.” The sequence به شمشیر یازید دست is an idiomatic, poetic ordering: به شمشیر (“toward/for the sword”), یازید (past verb ‘reached/ran/struck forth’) and دست “hand” — together meaning “he came and reached out his hand for the sword” (دست یازیدن = to stretch out one’s hand / to lay hands on).
segments.s451-g15: به زن گفت is the indirect object + verb “he said to the woman” (classical order: indirect object before verb). کژی و تاری مجوی is an admonition using the negative imperative or prohibitive sense of مجوی (“do not seek”): “do not seek crookedness (کژی) or darkness/shame (تاری).” The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s451-g16: هرآنچت بپرسم is a subordinate conditional/temporal phrase “whatever I ask of you” (هرآنچه + ـت). بپرسم here is subjunctive/optative first‑person form used in such dependent clauses (“if/when I ask”). سخن راست گوی is an instruction: سخن راست is “true speech,” and گوی is the imperative/verb “speak.” Together: “whatever I may ask, speak the truth.”
segments.s451-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. جنگ‌آوران را is a topical/definite NP (را marks it as definite/foregrounded). یکی گشت رای is a short clause where گشت is the past 'became' and یکی modifies رای: 'their counsel became one' (i.e. they agreed). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but follows classical Persian syntax: Conjunction + NP + predicate.
segments.s451-g90: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took/led'. لشکر is its direct object. ز پیش is a small prepositional phrase 'from before / in front of', and همای is a proper name (Homay). The verb–object order and compact phrasing are typical of poetic compression.
segments.s451-g91: فرستاد is a simple past 'he sent'. بیدار here functions as an adjective 'alert/awake' modifying کارآگهان ('informed people/agents'); the adjective precedes the noun for poetic emphasis (more commonly the adjective follows in modern colloquial Persian).
segments.s451-g92: بدان is analyzable as ب + دان here meaning 'to find out / to know' (i.e. 'to investigate'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that', نماند is the negative-form verb 'not remain/be left', and سخن در نهان = 'speech/talk in secret' (سخن 'talk', در نهان 'hidden/secret'). Together: 'he sent them to find out, so that no talk remained hidden.'
segments.s451-g93: ز نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase 'about/of the good and the bad' (ز = about/of). لشکر آگاه بود is a predicate 'the army (or he concerning the army) was aware' where آگاه is an adjectival predicate and بود is the past copula. The phrase states awareness with respect to both favorable and unfavorable aspects.
segments.s451-g94: ز بدها 'about the bad things' (ز = concerning), گمانیش is گمان + -ش 'his/its conjecture/suspicion', and کوتاه بود literally 'was short'—an idiom meaning his suspicion/expectation was small or not large. The clause comments that regarding bad matters his suspicion was limited.
segments.s451-g95: همی رفت is an iterative/imperfective past 'kept going' (همی marks continuous or habitual action). منزل به منزل is a reduplicative adverbial phrase 'from station to station / camp to camp' (منزل here = stage/camp). سپاه is the affected noun 'the army' (object/subject depending on context).
segments.s451-g96: This is a pair of verbless descriptive phrases: زمین پر سپاه = 'the earth full of hosts' and آسمان پر ز ماه = 'the sky full (because) of moon(s)'. پر after a noun means 'full (of)' and ز introduces the source/mark ('from/of the moon'). The lines are pictorial, using nominal phrases rather than finite clauses.
segments.s451-g17: شما را که باشم به گوهر کیم = A rhetorical question 'Who am I to you...'. شما را is the addressed pronoun with the object/definite marker را attached; که introduces the relative/clausal question 'who/that (I) am'; باشم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative ('that I be' / 'that I should be') used in Classical Persian for this kind of hypothetical/question; به + گوهر is a prepositional phrase 'to/with a jewel' (گوهر as 'gem'); کیم is an archaic interrogative 'who?' closing the question. Word order is fairly flexible: the relative clause (که باشم) modifies شما را and the prepositional phrase (به گوهر) describes the predicated status.
segments.s451-g18: به نزدیک گازر ز بهر چیم = A second question/pause that follows the first. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near, close to'; گازر is a noun 'passer/passerby' as the location/figure approached; ز here functions as the older poetic preposition 'from' (often written ز for از); بهر 'for/on behalf of' marks purpose/relation; چیم is an enclitic interrogative 'what of me?' or 'what have I (to do)?' The sequence asks why the speaker should draw near a passer for their own sake.
segments.s451-g19: زن گازر از بیم زنهار خواست = 'The passing woman, from fear, asked for zanehâr/mercy.' زن گازر is a noun phrase 'the woman who was passing/by'; از بیم is a prepositional phrase 'out of fear' (از + بیم); زنهار here is a noun 'a warning/plea/mercy' (classical sense often used as an entreaty); خواست is the simple past (3rd sg) 'asked/wanted' used here as 'begged/asked for'.
segments.s451-g20: خداوند داننده را یار خواست = 'She sought God, the Knower, as a helper.' خداوند داننده is a titling apposition 'the Lord/the All‑Knower' functioning as the object; را is the definite-object marker attached to the object phrase; یار is 'helper/ally/companion' and خواست is again past 3rd‑person 'asked/sought'. The verb agrees with the subject (the woman) understood from the previous clause.
segments.s451-g21: بدو گفت خون سر من مجوی = 'She said to him: Do not seek my blood.' بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb (typical Classical word order); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; خون سر من is a genitive phrase 'the blood of my head' (an idiom meaning 'my life' or 'my blood'); مجوی is the negative jussive/imperative form 'do not seek' (2nd‑person prohibition).
segments.s451-g22: بگویم ترا هرچ گفتی بگوی = 'I will tell you; say whatever you said/ask.' بگویم is 1st‑person present‑subjunctive/optative often used to mean 'I will tell' or a promise ('let me tell/ I will tell'); ت is the 2nd‑person enclitic object suffix ('you') attached as a separate token here; را is the object/definiteness marker; هرچ is 'whatever/anything'; گفتی is the 2nd‑person past (classical use) 'you said/asked'; بگوی is an exhortative/imperative form 'say!' or 'speak!' The clause invites the addressee to speak or ask and promises the speaker will answer.
segments.s451-g23: سخنها یکایک بر و بر شمرد = 'He counted the words one by one, again and again.' سخنها is the plural 'the words/speeches'; یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each in turn'; بر و بر is a repetitive adverbial phrase ('again and again' / 'one after another'); شمرد is the past‑tense verb 'he counted/reckoned'. Classical Persian frequently stacks adverbs before the verb as here.
segments.s451-g24: بکوشید وز کار کژی نبرد = 'He strove so that no crooked deed would come of the business.' بکوشید is past 'he strove/endeavoured' (3rd sg); وز here is an older/poetic connector (function akin to 'از' = 'from' / 'with') and is somewhat idiomatic; کار کژی is a genitive group 'a crooked/wrong deed' or 'wrongness of the affair'; نبرد here functions in the clause as a resultative idea 'did not come about' / 'would not arise' (classical usage where the lexical items map to 'not-carry/bring about'). The whole clause expresses intention/result — he tried to prevent any wrong deed.

sentences.s451-s13: From the chest and from the nursing infant, from dinars and from royal gems.
sentences.s451-s14: He said to him, 'We are workmen; we do not give as those of a fortunate lineage.'
sentences.s451-s15: From that we have something for you that exists — a garment to wear — and he put it on.
sentences.s451-s16: We are your devotees and the command is yours; consider what body and life should be yours.
sentences.s451-s1: One day he said to the passerby thus: "I always keep this secret from the assembly."
sentences.s451-s2: May my love for you never waver; may my face remain upon your face, as your face upon mine.
sentences.s451-s3: It astonishes me when we call someone a son and seat him in his own shop.
sentences.s451-s4: The passer said to him: "This speech is regrettable — it has brought back the old sufferings."
sentences.s451-s41: It appears that this renowned man was wise and a warlike horseman.
sentences.s451-s42: He is brave, proud, and resourceful, but his weapon is not suitable.
sentences.s451-s43: When Farmand came to Darab, the army pleased him completely.
sentences.s451-s44: From the stars, on a certain day, (fate) chose him for the rank of spahbed, just as he deserved.
sentences.s451-s21: He had given him so many dinars; that costly jewel nevertheless remained of no avail.
sentences.s451-s22: With the dinars he bought a steed he liked; one (item) a cheap saddle and the other a halter.
sentences.s451-s23: One (of them) was a marzban (border-warden), with gems and counsel — great, esteemed, and a guide.
sentences.s451-s24: Darab strode proudly near him; anxious in thought, ill at heart and gloomy was he.
sentences.s451-s33: When the host became numerous, troops came from every gate and from every quarter.
sentences.s451-s34: From the august palace came Humay himself and the frontier-guards, neat of appearance.
sentences.s451-s35: So that the army might pass before him, and they might count bodies, names and the registers.
sentences.s451-s36: They remained for a while on that broad plain; when the numerous host went forth, it passed on.
sentences.s451-s29: There was a certain man of ill repute — Rashnavad; he was an ill‑famed spahbed as well, called Sepahbednzhad.
sentences.s451-s30: He commanded that they march toward Rome, and with the sword lay waste the land.
sentences.s451-s31: Rashnavad then mustered the army; he established a place of audience and set a day.
sentences.s451-s32: When Darab heard, he became joyful; he went up to him and inscribed (wrote down) the name.
sentences.s451-s25: The noble marzban kept him continually; from the world no direct harm came.
sentences.s451-s26: It was so bad that an army came from Rûm to plunder that prosperous border land.
sentences.s451-s27: In the fighting the marzban was slain within; the head of his host, on hearing that news, was struck (overcome).
sentences.s451-s28: When the news reached Homay that the Romans had set foot on this border.
sentences.s451-s17: When Darab heard, he stood staring, and he set his soul into thought.
sentences.s451-s18: He said to him: 'From this request nothing remains; but if a report spreads it, it will scatter it all.'
sentences.s451-s19: That the price of a single leaf should be, on this day, slowness and misery.
sentences.s451-s20: Thus he answered that it is worth more than this flourishing tree, the garden, and the land.
sentences.s451-s37: When he saw Darab, with farr and exaltation, he had a steel mace hung about his neck.
sentences.s451-s38: You said, 'The whole plain is his breadth; the earth lies beneath the marcher and his height is above.'
sentences.s451-s39: When he saw that glory and pleasant countenance, the lion was nursed at its mother's breast.
sentences.s451-s40: He inquired and said, 'Where is this rider from? — (he is) with this horn, this exaltation, and the high right (i.e. commanding the right/upper place).'
sentences.s451-s5: If your origin is from that, mine is superior; as for the seeker of a father, the secret lies with the mother.
sentences.s451-s6: So ill (was it) that one day the passer departed; from the house toward the river he burst forth, blazing.
sentences.s451-s7: Darab shut the house’s door tightly; he came and stretched out his hand toward the sword.
sentences.s451-s8: He said to the woman: seek neither crookedness nor shame; whatever I ask, speak the truth.
sentences.s451-s45: When the warriors' counsel became one, they led the army from before Homay.
sentences.s451-s46: He sent alert, well-informed agents to find out, so that no talk remained hidden.
sentences.s451-s47: He (or they) was aware of the army's good and bad; concerning the bad his suspicion was slight.
sentences.s451-s48: He (or the army) kept going from camp to camp; the earth was full of hosts, the sky full of moons.
sentences.s451-s9: Who am I to you that I should be a jewel? Why should I draw near a passer for my own sake?
sentences.s451-s10: The passing woman, out of fear, begged for mercy; she asked the Lord, the All‑Knower, for aid.
sentences.s451-s11: She said to him, 'Do not seek my blood; I'll tell you — say whatever you wish.'
sentences.s451-s12: He counted the words one by one, over and over, and strove that no crooked deed arise from the matter.
